title: Credit supply and house prices: Evidence from mortgage market segmentation
abstract: This paper develops a difference-in-differences estimator that uses annual changes in the conforming loan limit and the 80% loan-to-value (LTV) threshold to isolate the impact of easier access to credit on house prices. Houses that become eligible for financing with an 80% LTV conforming loan increase in value by about $1.17 per square foot, controlling for a rich set of characteristics. Our estimates imply a local elasticity of house prices to interest rates below 6, which suggests that interest rates are capitalized into prices to a lesser extent than proposed by studies relying on more aggregate variation.

author.Manuel Adelino: Duke University, United States of America|||NBER, United States of America
author.Antoinette Schoar*: MIT, United States of America|||NBER, United States of America
author.Felipe Severino: Dartmouth College, United States of America

article.1. Introduction: A fundamental question in finance involves understanding the impact of easier access to credit and lower financing costs on asset prices. In the housing market, easier credit conditions have been associated with pronounced house price increases and subsequent reversals when credit dries up. This dynamic received considerable attention in the context of the US housing boom of the early 2000s, and earlier work suggests that supply-side factors such as lower interest rates, financial innovation, securitization, and reductions in collateral constraints contributed to the boom. However, the literature has also shown that these shifts in credit access and cost went hand in hand with changes in market sentiment and expectations about future appreciation, which in turn increased asset prices and relaxed credit constraints through increased collateral values.3 A key difficulty in this literature is to establish the direction of causality between credit conditions and prices: How does easier access to credit get capitalized into house prices holding constant house price expectations, demand for housing and improved collateral values?In this paper, we develop a difference-in-differences estimator that uses annual changes in the conforming loan limit (CLL) to generate exogenous variation in the cost and availability of credit for individual housing transactions. A key advantage of this approach is that, duringthe time period we consider, the charters of Fannie Mae and Freddie Mac required that annual changes in the CLL be equal to the growth in the national single family house price index, and so were unrelated to local housing markets and the local economy.4 This generates exogenous variation in the set of houses that can be financed more easily using a conforming loan. We find that a home that is easy to finance with a conforming loan, on average, has a $1.17 higher value per square foot compared to a house that falls just outside the range of CLL funding. This result forms the basis of our estimate of how lower interest rates get capitalized into house prices.The CLL determines the maximum size of a mortgage that can be purchased or securitized by Fannie Mae or Freddie Mac. This implicit (and since 2008, explicit) government support for loans below the conforming loan limit reduces the cost of borrowing and provides better access to credit for a wide range of borrowers. The difference in interest rates between conforming and jumbo loans (those above the conforming limit) during our sample period (before the 2008 financial crisis) was estimated to be up to 24 basis points (McKenzie, 2002, Ambrose et al., 2004, Sherlund, 2008, Kaufman, 2014, DeFusco and Paciorek, 2017). Loutskina and Strahan, 2009, Loutskina and Strahan, 2011 and DeFusco and Paciorek (2017) show that lenders screen borrowers for jumbo loans more intensely than they do for those that apply for conforming loans, which implies that mortgages below the conforming loan limit are not only cheaper but also easier to obtain.To identify the latent set of properties that are “easy” to finance with a conforming loan in a given year, we divide the conforming loan limit by 0.8, a widely used loan-to-value (LTV) threshold in the industry (Green and Wachter, 2005).5 By construction, houses below this threshold can get a conforming loan for 80 percent of the price of the house, whereas houses that transact above 125 percent of the CLL cannot. For transactions above this price threshold, borrowers either have to finance their home with an 80 percent mortgage using a jumbo loan at a higher interest rate or, if they want to take advantage of the lower interest rate below the CLL, they must use savings or alternative forms of financing to make a larger down payment. Either way, transactions above the CLL face a more constrained access to finance and a higher cost of capital.To assess the impact of easier access to conforming loans on house prices, we run differences-in-differences regressions in which we compare transactions just above the threshold of 125% of the CLL in a given year to properties that fall below the threshold in the subsequent year, after the limit has been increased and can now be financed with an 80% conforming loan. The intuition is that the transactions above the threshold are harder to finance and thus their prices have been bid up less relative to the underlying fundamentals. An increase in the conforming loan limit should affect this part of the house price distribution more strongly since it allows new potential borrowers to enter this segment of the market. In addition, since house price levels differ markedly across regions in the United States, the change in the CLL affects different parts of the housing stock and different borrowers across areas depending on the local price level. This rules out the possibility that differential trends in prices and income of a particular type of house and borrower could explain the results. Finally, we cannot use a simple regression discontinuity design within a given year because households can choose whether to obtain a jumbo loan or “stretch” and increase the down payment to remain below the limit. By using all properties around the 125% threshold, independent of how they are financed, our approach is akin to an intent-to-treat design and is immune from bias due to the endogenous choice of financing of a specific transaction.We start by confirming that the CLL and the 80% cutoff affect borrowers’ choice of financing. The norm in the mortgage market during this period was to borrow at a first mortgage LTV of exactly 0.8 (about 60% of transactions have an LTV of 0.8). For house purchases just above 125% of the CLL, a much larger fraction of transactions is at an LTV below 80% and at exactly the conforming loan limit, confirming that the CLL is also a key determinant of mortgage finance. As we mention above, these borrowers may use a conforming loan either because the combination of a conforming loan and other forms of financing (savings or other forms of debt) is cheaper than a jumbo loan, or because they are excluded from the jumbo loan market altogether.Using the exogenous variation generated by the annual CLL changes, we then show that transactions just above 125% of the CLL, that is, those that are “harder” to finance, occur at lower values per square foot than those below the threshold. To increase the comparability of house characteristics, we focus on houses that transact within a band of $10,000 dollars around the threshold of the conforming loan limit divided by 0.8. Because we look locally around the threshold, all of the transactions above the CLL that we include in a given year would be eligible to be financed at 80 percent LTV in the subsequent year, i.e. after the limit was raised. We observe a $1.17 discount per square foot for a mean value per square foot of $220 (i.e., about 53 basis points of the average house value). This difference is reduced to $0.67 per square foot (30 basis points) after we control for house characteristics, suggesting that part of the effect we find can be accounted for by differences in the observable quality of houses above and below the threshold. These effects are significantly different from those we obtain when we use placebo loan limits elsewhere in the distribution, confirming that we are picking up the causal effect of the CLL.Our findings are qualitatively unchanged when we use two additional measures of the property value to account for underlying differences in house quality: (1) the residuals of the logarithm of house prices from a hedonic regression using a large set of controls for the underlying characteristics of the house and (2) the residuals of the value per square foot from similar hedonic regressions. We run the hedonic regressions by year and by metropolitan statistical area, and use the set of controls available from deeds registries. Controls include not only common variables, such as the number of rooms and bedrooms, but also details about the type of heating, architectural type, and building type, among many other variables.For our main specifications, we assign transactions to the treatment and control groups using transaction prices. We confirm that our results are robust to assigning the treatment based on ex-ante predicted house values using hedonic regressions. The effects are quantitatively and qualitatively unchanged. Furthermore, we show that our results are not driven by a focal point effect, whereby more bidders are drawn in to bid on houses just around the threshold. First, there is no visible bunching in the number of transactions just around the CLL divided by 0.8, suggesting that the supply of houses on the market does not strongly react to the CLL. We also do not find bunching in the number of unobserved bidders for homes around the CLL, which we measure as the share of borrowers who apply for loans but ultimately either withdraw or do not use the loans they are approved for. If the CLL were just a focal point for listing and sales values, we should expect more bidders who are eligible for conforming loans. Instead, we find that our measure of the share of failed bids is lower for borrowers below CLL, inconsistent with the notion that the CLL/0.8 is an important focal point for bidding.Given our estimate of the change in house prices due to the ease of financing with a conforming loan, we can compute a semi-elasticity of prices to differences in interest rates near the threshold. As we have previously described, easier access to financing due to the conforming loan limit represents a bundled treatment that varies among borrowers based on their available outside options. To estimate a semi-elasticity, we focus on a borrower transitioning from a conforming loan to a jumbo loan, and we use the difference in interest rates between the two kinds of loans of 10 to 24 basis points estimated in the prior literature. For buyers moving from a conforming loan below the threshold to a jumbo loan above, we obtain local elasticity estimates ranging from 1.3 to 5.3. These elasticity estimates are at the lower end of what prior research has found, suggesting a relatively muted pass-through of changes in interest rates to house prices (a point also made by Glaeser et al., 2013). While this is a local estimate of the elasticity around the conforming loan limit for borrowers moving from the conforming to the jumbo loan market, it provides one of the first empirical evaluations of how easier access to financing due to the availability of conforming loans is capitalized into house prices. These findings also suggest that a portion of the benefits of easier access to credit is passed onto homeowners in the form of increased house prices.Our paper contributes to a large literature exploring several aspects of the aggregate relationship between changes in credit conditions and house prices. Hubbard and Mayer, 2009, Davis et al., 2020, and Justiniano et al. (2022), among others, highlight the role of lower interest rates in driving house prices. Calomiris, 2009, Mian and Sufi, 2009, and Pavlov and Wachter (2011) discuss the role of financial innovation and securitization, and Khandani et al., 2013, Favilukis et al., 2017, Corbae and Quintin, 2015, Landvoigt et al., 2015, Greenwald, 2018, DeFusco, 2018, and Zevelev (2021) are some examples of work focusing on how reductions in collateral constraints affect the housing cycle. While the focus of this line of work is on the role of easier credit on house prices, an influential set of studies has argued that easier credit is often accompanied or even preceded by changes in market sentiment and expectations about future appreciation and that credit alone cannot explain the large boom in prices witnessed during the housing bubble of the early 2000s (Shiller, 2008, Glaeser, 2013, Glaeser et al., 2013, Adelino et al., 2016, Nathanson and Zwick, 2018, DeFusco et al., 2022). Many prominent papers have discussed the broader relationship between credit, asset prices and the creation of bubbles (seminal papers include Kiyotaki and Moore, 1997, Allen and Gale, 1998, Bernanke and Gertler, 2001, Case and Shiller, 2003, Kindleberger et al., 2005, Geanakoplos, 2010, Brunnermeier et al., 2012).Closer to the specific question we address in this paper, Favara and Imbs (2015) use exposure to bank branching deregulation as an instrument for credit-supply shocks at the state level to quantify the effect on house prices. Di Maggio and Kermani (2017) use variation in anti-predatory lending laws at the state level to quantify the impact of boom and bust in house prices resulting from credit-supply shocks.6 Ours is the first paper to use micro-level supply shocks to identify a causal link between interest rates and house prices during normal times. Our focus on individual-level transactions allows us to more precisely quantify a range of house price elasticities to inform the relationship between credit supply and asset prices. Argyle et al. (2021) explore the role of a credit supply shock on the price of durable goods with a focus on the maturity of loans. Kaufman (2014) uses the price divided by 80% threshold for appraisal values to study the effect of the conforming status of a loan on its cost and contract structure. Loutskina and Strahan (2015) follow our approach by using changes in the CLL to shock the availability of credit at the regional level to investigate financial integration and the propagation of shocks.
article.2. The user cost model: The existing literature has focused on different versions of the user-cost model (Poterba, 1984) to draw conclusions about the impact of changes in the cost of credit on the price of housing. In this model, the mortgage interest rate is the main determinant of the cost of owning and agents are indifferent between owning and renting if the housing market is in equilibrium. However, the magnitude of the elasticity between mortgage rates and house prices depends on a number of different assumptions about other parameters, such as borrowers’ private discount factors or expectations about future mortgage rates. Since many of these parameters are not observable, it is important to get a precise estimate of the impact of the cost of credit on prices.To fix the intuition, we follow Glaeser et al. (2013) and describe the basic elements of the user cost model. This result is analogous to the setup in Hubbard and Mayer (2009) as well as a simplified version of the user cost in Himmelberg et al. (2005). Renting a property involves paying rent equal to Rt in each period. Owning a property, on the other hand, includes making a down payment θ that is a proportion of the price of the house Pt and obtaining a mortgage that is rolled over each period, such that principal is never completely paid down. The borrower pays interest on the mortgage at a rate rt that is deflated by the relevant tax rate ϕ, as well as property taxes and maintenance costs equal to τ. Both rents and house maintenance costs grow at a rate of g. The model assumes that individuals have a private discount factor of ρt. If we assume that market interest rates and private discount rates are constant and equal to each other (i.e., ρ=r), we can write the indifference condition for users as (1)RtPt=(1−ϕ)r−g+τ.If the assumptions of this model hold, changes in the user cost (the right-hand side of the equation) lead to changes in the equilibrium price-to-rent ratio. For example, if the user cost is 5%, then the price of a house should be about 20 times its market rent. In such a world, a drop of one percentage point in mortgage rates would lead to a decline of (1−τ) in the user cost, or 0.75, if we assume a marginal tax rate of 25%. The price-to-rent ratio then would be 23.5, which is an increase in the price of 17.5% (an elasticity of 17.5). This magnitude of elasticities is proposed in Himmelberg et al. (2005) and Hubbard and Mayer (2009) to explain both the time series and the cross-section of house prices in the previous decades.Glaeser et al. (2013) show that a more realistic version of the user cost model produces lower elasticities of prices to interest rates than the simple model. In particular, if private discount rates are not the same as market rates, changes in interest rates will not mechanically alter how users discount future expected house price appreciation. This change alone can reduce the elasticity to just 8, instead of the initial 17.5. Other mechanisms through which the elasticity could be substantially reduced include mean-reverting interest rates, which means borrowers anticipate having to sell a home at a time when rates are higher, or the possibility of prepaying a mortgage. Our econometric approach allows us to more carefully identify the magnitude of the change in house prices due to changes in the average cost of financing due to the conforming loan limit, since we look at movements in the cost of capital for home buyers that are exogenous to local conditions and to individual transactions. Our empirical results provide local estimates for the numerator of the elasticity calculation. In Section 5.3, we discuss the range of elasticities that is consistent with our results.Table 1. article.3. Data and methodology: The data set we use in this paper contains all the ownership transfers of residential properties available in deeds and assessors’ records over 11 years, from 1998 to 2008, and 74 counties in 10 metropolitan statistical areas (MSAs): Boston, Chicago, DC, Denver, Las Vegas, Los Angeles, Miami, New York, San Diego, and San Francisco. We limit our attention to transactions of single-family houses, which account for the large majority (approximately 78%) of all observations.Each observation in the data contains the date of the transaction, the amount for which a house was sold, the size of the first mortgage, and an extensive set of variables about the property itself. These characteristics include the property address, interior square footage, lot size, number of bedrooms, number of bathrooms, total rooms, house age, renovation status, and date of renovation. Additional characteristics include the availability of a fireplace, parking, the architectural and structural style of the building, the type of construction, exterior material, availability of heating or cooling, heating and cooling mechanism, type of roof, view, attic, basement, and garage. In the Online Appendix, we describe the procedure for cleaning the raw data received from Dataquick.3.1. Summary statisticsTable 1, Table 2 summarize the 3.96 million transactions of single-family houses that make up the data set used in this paper. The Online Appendix contains a detailed description of the procedure for cleaning the data initially obtained from Dataquick and how to arrive at this number of observations. Panel A of Table 1 shows that the average transaction value is 309 thousand dollars with a standard deviation of 124 thousand dollars. The average size of the houses is 1733 square feet, and the houses have, on average, 3 bedrooms and 2 bathrooms. The average loan-to-value is 0.81 (including only the first mortgage for each transaction), and the median LTV is exactly 80%. The average value per square foot is $194 with a standard deviation of $92 per square foot (first row of Panel B).Table 1 also shows the summary statistics for the sample we use in the regressions in the final three columns. For the regression sample, the average price for each house is higher than in the whole data set (at 371 thousand dollars, compared to 309 thousand dollars in the first column). This is consistent with the fact that the conforming loan limit was set to cover substantially more than 50% of the mortgages made every year (Acharya et al., 2011). These houses are also, on average, larger and have more bedrooms and bathrooms than the whole data set.Panel A of Table 2 shows marked differences in the summary statistics for each of the 10 MSAs included in our data. San Francisco is the metropolitan area with the highest average house price, at 384 thousand dollars. Denver is the area with the lowest valuation, with an average of approximately 250 thousand dollars. When we compare values per square foot, we find a similar result, namely, San Francisco touts the highest valuation with an average of $267 per square foot, and Las Vegas affords the lowest valuation with an average of $137 per square foot.Table 2, Panel B, shows the trend of nominal house prices over time. Here, one can see the increase in house prices from an average of 240 thousand USD in 1998 to a peak of 367 thousand USD in 2006, as well as the increase in the volume of transactions over the same period. Values per square foot follow a similar pattern. The loan-to-value average (including only the first mortgage) is stable, both across MSAs and throughout time at around 0.8.Table 2. Hedonic regressionOne of the advantages of using deeds registry data is the richness of the information available about property characteristics. The depth of information allows us to account for price differences between houses on the basis of observable features. Specifically, we assess whether the observed price impact due to changes in the conforming loan limit is attributable to differences in house quality, or if these differences persist even after accounting for quality.To distinguish between these two explanations, we estimate hedonic regressions of value per square foot and the logarithm of house prices on a variety of house characteristics and estimate the residuals for each of these two left-hand-side variables (which we denote by LHSi). Specifically, we estimate the following regressions by MSA and by year: (2)LHSi=γ0+ΓXi+monthi+zipcodei+ɛi.By estimating these regressions by year and by MSA, we allow the coefficients for the characteristics to vary along these two dimensions. We also use monthly indicator variables to account for seasonality in the housing market, as well as ZIP code fixed effects. The set of controls Xi is a similar set of controls to that used in Campbell et al. (2011), with some additional characteristics. The controls include square footage, high and low square footage dummies, the size of the lot, the number of bedrooms and bathrooms, and a number of indicators for interior and exterior house characteristics (e.g., fireplace, style of the building). We describe which variables are included, as well as details about the construction of each variable, in the Online Appendix.The estimated R2 of each of these regressions (80 in total for each of the two left-hand-side variable; 10 MSAs in 8 years) is between 40% and 60% for the transaction price, and 50% to 70% when we use value per square foot as the dependent variable.Panel B of Table 1 records the summary statistics for the residuals from the hedonic regressions for the whole sample. The average of the residuals is, by construction, zero. The standard deviation of the errors is about $42/sq ft. The hedonic regressions are estimated on the whole data set of transactions, so when we restrict our attention to the regression sample, the average error is no longer zero. Indeed, for the regression sample, the average residual from the hedonic regressions for the value per square foot is positive at $5.3, and the average error for the logarithm of the transaction value of the house is $0.05 (last three columns of Panel B of Table 1).3.3. Empirical approachTo identify the effect of changes in credit conditions due to easier access to conforming loans on house prices, we restrict our analysis to two groups of transactions within tight price range that differ in the financing available to them. The regression sample consists of houses that transact in a band around 125% of each year’s conforming loan limit, as well as houses from the following year in the same price range. Specifically, we divide houses into two groups: houses below the threshold of 125% of the year’s CLL (i.e., transactions that fall between 125% of the CLL and 125% of the CLL minus $10,000) and houses above the threshold that transact between 125% of the CLL and 125% of the CLL+$10,000.By construction, in the year that the conforming loan limit is in effect, houses above the threshold of 125% of the CLL cannot be financed at 80% using a conforming loan, whereas the houses below the threshold can be financed. Thus, home buyers who bid for houses priced above 125% of the CLL cannot finance a full 80% of the transaction with the cheaper and more easily available conforming loans. In the subsequent year, the CLL is raised, and both groups of transactions can be financed at 80% with a conforming loan.7 Our sample includes all transactions in this price range, regardless of the mortgage choice made by each buyer. This ensures our estimates are not biased by the endogenous financing choice of each specific transaction.Consider, for instance, the year 1999: In that year, the conforming loan limit (CLL) for single-family houses was $240,000. The corresponding threshold for houses that year is $300,000 (1.25×240,000). In that year, houses above the threshold have prices between $300,000 and $310,000, and houses below the threshold have a transaction price between $290,000 and $300,000 (those that transact at exactly $300,000 are included in this second group). In our main regressions, we track these two groups of houses from 1999 to 2000, with 1999 being the year the CLL is in effect, and 2000 being the year all these transactions could be bought using a conforming loan at a full 80% LTV. Indeed, the CLL changed in 2000 to $252,700, so the threshold of 125% of the CLL was $315,875, and our above-the-threshold group for 1999 could now access an 80% LTV conforming loan.One important assumption in our analysis is that borrowers in the group above the threshold of 125% of the CLL are constrained in their choice of financing. To maintain an LTV of 0.8, borrowers have two alternatives: The first is to take a jumbo loan, which on average is between 10 and 24 basis points more expensive than a conforming loan (McKenzie, 2002, Ambrose et al., 2004, Sherlund, 2008; Kaufman, 2014; DeFusco and Paciorek, 2017). Alternatively, borrowers can borrow up to the conforming loan limit and cover the rest of the house price with savings or other forms of debt, depending on the best available outside option. The latter means having a first mortgage LTV of less than 80% and accessing additional sources of funding that are likely substantially more expensive than the conforming mortgage rate. Since originating banks perform more careful screening before awarding jumbo loans (Loutskina and Strahan, 2009, Loutskina and Strahan, 2011), obtaining a conforming loan and making a larger down payment may be the only option for some borrowers as they may be excluded from the jumbo market altogether. Fig. 6 illustrates how the cost of capital evolves for borrowers with and without access to the jumbo loan market as a function of the price of a transaction. In the graph we focus on the cost of financing 80% of the value of a home and we assume that the conforming loan rate is the lowest cost capital available to borrowers (an assumption that is at the core of our identification strategy and is supported by previous literature, e.g., Green and Wachter, 2005). We write the transaction price as a proportion of the CLL (i.e., Price=m⋅CLL), to emphasize that the average cost of funding rises above the conforming loan rate at the threshold of 1.25⋅CLL. At this house price level, borrowers can still obtain an 80% LTV conforming loan, and right above this price they start putting down additional equity to purchase the homes (before eventually switching to a jumbo loan).Download: Download high-res image (422KB)Download: Download full-size imageFig. 1. Transaction-loan value surface . This figure shows the frequency of transactions at each house price-loan value combination for the year 2000 and 2004, and the 10 MSAs covered in our data, where both house prices and loan values were binned at $10,000 intervals. The mass of transactions on the diagonal have a loan-to-value of approximately 0.8.If we call the cost of additional funds rDP (for down payment) and the conforming loan rate rc, the weighted average annual cost in dollars to finance 80% of the value of a home taking into account the additional down payment is: rc⋅CLL+rDP⋅(0.8⋅m⋅CLL−CLL)Note that m⋅CLL is the transaction price as a function of the conforming loan limit, so the buyer needs to put down (0.8⋅m⋅CLL−CLL) additional dollars of down payment to reach 80% of the value of the home. If, instead, the borrower chooses to use a jumbo loan at a rate rj for the same LTV of 80%, the cost in dollars is: rj⋅0.8⋅m⋅CLLIf we re-write the expression in terms of the jumbo-conforming spread rj−c=rj−rc and the spread of the cost of the down payment over the conforming spread rDP−c=rDP−rc, after re-arranging terms we can see that the borrower chooses a jumbo loan if: rj−c≤rDP−c⋅(1−10.8⋅m)Borrowers who can access the jumbo loan market choose between the cost of the additional down payment and funding the full 80% of the price with a jumbo loan. For this subset of borrowers, the spread in the cost of capital over the conforming loan rate levels out at the jumbo-conforming spread rj−c. If borrowers cannot access the jumbo loan market, the spread over the conforming loan rate increases even past this level and may, in fact, be significantly higher than the 10 to 24 basis points mentioned above.The slope of the increase in the weighted cost of capital varies by borrower, as it is a function of the cheapest available source of additional down payment. Fig. 6 plots a borrower with a low cost of additional funds in blue (labeled rLowDP in the graph) and one with a higher cost of funds in red (rHighDP). The cost of the best available option for additional funds for a down payment is likely to be significantly higher than the cost of a first mortgage for the vast majority of home buyers. This is the case whether the source is savings or other forms of debt, for example, second lien mortgages. Note, in addition, that the slope of the increase in cost may change as the borrower switches across sources of funds. For example, a borrower may initially use savings and then second liens or other forms of financing as the down payment requirement increases. However, irrespective of whether borrowers choose a jumbo loan or make up the difference to the CLL using other sources of funding, their financing options become less attractive as the transaction amount exceeds 125% of the limit, compared to below it.3.3.1. Empirical specificationOur main regressions estimate the size of the effect of the constraint imposed by the conforming loan limit on the valuation of transactions that occur just above the threshold of 125% of the CLL. We run difference-in-differences regressions year by year with one indicator variable for houses priced above the conforming loan limit divided by 0.8, another indicator for the year in which the CLL is in effect (effectively the “pre” year), and the interaction of these two indicator variables. We also include ZIP code fixed effects in all regressions, so our estimates do not reflect differences between neighborhoods, but rather variation within ZIP codes.The sample for each year-by-year regression includes houses within a $10,000 band around the conforming loan limit in the year when the limit is in force, as well as the subsequent year. The “Above the threshold” indicator variable takes a value of one if the price at which a house transacts is greater than 125% of the conforming loan limit of a certain year and less than that amount plus $10,000. This variable is zero for transactions between 125% of the CLL and 125% of the CLL minus $10,000. The “Year CLL” indicator variable is set to one in the year when the CLL is in effect for each regression and zero in the subsequent year.We employ a tight band around the threshold so that all transactions in the year after the limit is in effect are eligible for an 80% LTV conforming loan. We thus have a group of transactions that is “easy” to finance and another one that is “difficult” to finance in the year in which the limit is in effect, but all transactions in the sample are “easy” to finance once the limit is raised.8 We run regressions of the following form: (3)Valuationi=β1×AboveThresholdi+β2×YearCLLi+β3×AboveThresholdi×YearCLL+γZIP+ɛi.We estimate this regression for each year between 1998 and 2005. We cannot include 2006 and 2007 because the national conforming loan limit did not change after 2006 in our sample period (house prices dropped and the administration left the limit unchanged). We also do not analyze the changes that were made to conforming loan limits in 2008 in high-cost areas as part of the Housing and Economic Recovery Act of 2008; the limit was chosen by the government, as opposed to being mechanically related to previous limits, so this introduces the possibility that the “jumbo conforming” program was designed to assist specific areas and thus would be endogenous to expected future appreciation.After we obtain β1, β2, and β3 for all 8 years (1998–2005), we estimate Fama–MacBeth averages (Fama and MacBeth, 1973) of these coefficients and obtain the standard errors of these averages by using the standard deviation of the estimated coefficients and dividing the standard deviation by the square root of the number of coefficients. We test the robustness of our results to serial correlation in the error term. We do so by constructing Newey–West standard errors and find that all the results are unchanged.We also run a pooled version of these regressions as an alternative to the Fama–MacBeth procedure described above. To understand the pooled specification, it is useful to review how the regression sample is created. Each change in the CLL defines a specific set of transactions associated with that year and the next. For example, the change in CLL from 1998 of $277,150 to the 1999 limit of $240,000 defines a set of houses around the 1998 threshold (1.25×CLL, or $346,438 in 1998). In 1998, the houses below this threshold are eligible to be funded with an 80% conforming loan, but houses that transact above are not. However, houses around the same 1998 threshold in 1999 are all eligible to be financed with a conforming loan because of the change in the conforming loan limit. All transactions in 1998 and 1999 within this $10,000 band around the threshold are assigned to a 1998 event-cohort indicator. We then combine the transaction samples for all years in a pooled specification that includes event-cohort fixed effects interacted with the specification in (3), with the exception of AboveThreshold×YearCLL. The pooled specification is given by: (4)Valuationi,c=β1×AboveThresholdi×γc+β2×YearCLLi×γc+β3×AboveThresholdi×YearCLLi+γZIP×γc+νi,c.Where γc is an event-cohort indicator variable that is equal to one if transaction i belongs to the sample defined by the conforming loan limit in year c (for cohort, where c represents years 1998 through 2005 in which the CLL is in effect). The sample therefore includes the same set of transactions as the original specification. The coefficient of interest that can be compared to the Fama–MacBeth estimates is β3. In effect, the pooled specification produces a different weighting of the coefficients relative to the Fama–MacBeth method, but the nature of the specification is the same. Standard errors are clustered at the event-cohort level.Our approach is not a regression discontinuity design but rather a differences-in-differences design for each pair of years. We take this approach for a couple of reasons: First, the threshold that we use does not imply a sharp discontinuity in the ease of financing a home. For a house just one dollar above the threshold, a home buyer can still obtain a conforming mortgage by simply providing one additional dollar of equity, thereby keeping the total cost of financing the house nearly unchanged (as we discuss in detail in the previous section when we describe Fig. 6). As we move progressively away from the threshold, transactions become harder to finance. For our differences-in-differences estimator to be valid, all we need is that houses above the threshold are somewhat harder to finance, though not necessarily discontinuously so.The second reason for not using a regression discontinuity design is that in the year that the limit is in effect, home buyers choose to buy houses above or below the threshold, meaning the position with respect to the limit is not exogenous. On the contrary, our differences-in-differences specification uses the exogenous change in the conforming loan limit to compare a group of transactions that are above the limit in a year, but below in the next with a group of transaction that are always below the limit. This approach achieves a clean identification of the effect of credit availability on house prices.Our identification strategy can account for the possibility of differential growth rates within the distribution of house types across the country. Differential growth rates affect different parts of the housing stock across areas depending on the price level of the area, so the use of one threshold for the whole country mitigates concerns that one “special” type of home buyer drives our estimate.
article.4. Evidence on financing choices and eff: As pointed out earlier, the first stage in our empirical strategy is to demonstrate that the changes in the conforming loan limit significantly affect the financing choices of borrowers. As Fig. 1 shows, the most frequent choice on the part of borrowers is to have an LTV of exactly 80% (represented by the large mass along the diagonal of the figure). This holds also for jumbo loans, i.e. those above the conforming loan limit. The main exception to this rule occurs exactly at the conforming loan limit, where a significant mass of borrowers chooses an LTV below 0.8 by sticking to a conforming loan (in 2000, the limit was $252,700, and, in 2004, it was $333,700, and we can see a significant mass at these points in Panels (a) and (b) of Fig. 1). The data show that in the year in which the CLL is in effect, about 45% of the houses below the threshold in our sample are bought with an LTV of exactly 80%. For houses just above this boundary just 19% of borrowers pick 80% LTVs, which means using a jumbo loan for these transactions. Additionally, on average 55% of the transactions just above the threshold are financed using a conforming loan, which means having an LTV lower than 80%. These borrowers end up with an LTV of 77%–79.5%, which is a very infrequent choice anywhere else in the distribution. Again, these borrowers might have a lower LTV because they choose to stay below the CLL due to the cost of the loan or because they are excluded from the jumbo market altogether. Whatever the reason, this group of borrowers is “constrained” in the set of options available to them for financing their house. We plot the fraction of borrowers choosing a jumbo loan around the threshold of 1.25 x CLL in Figure 2 of the Online Appendix.In Fig. 2, we focus on the groups of transactions that we include in the regressions. The first panel tracks transactions up to $10,000 below 125% of the conforming loan limit in each year, whereas the second panel includes transactions up to $10,000 above the threshold. We show the total number of transactions (for all years between 1998 and 2006) in each month during the year prior to the limit being in effect, in the year that the limit is valid, and in the subsequent year.The main message from Fig. 2 is that in the year that the CLL is in effect, the composition of financing choices by borrowers differs very significantly from the other years. The 80% group becomes very prominent for the transactions below 125% of the CLL, whereas it is small for the transactions above the threshold. At the same time, the borrowers who stick with a conforming loan and buy houses above 125% of the CLL become an important fraction of all borrowers (they have an LTV between 75% and 79.5%). In the year after the limit is in effect, the choice of LTV between the two groups becomes indistinguishable from one another.Download: Download high-res image (761KB)Download: Download full-size imageFig. 2. Panel A uses year-by-year regressions and the Fama–MacBeth procedure, whereas Panel B shows the results of the pooled regressions discussed in Section 3.3.1. This effect is statistically and economically significant given how little variation there is in the modal choice of LTV of borrowers. The second column of both Panels in Table 3 shows that borrowers also obtain, on average, smaller loans in the year that the limit is in effect and when the price of the house is above the threshold, consistent with the result on LTVs.4.1. Number of transactionsThere are several reasons to expect quantities to change due to differential cost of credit, including different levels of down-payment (Stein, 1995) or sellers waiting for buyers to obtain better credit conditions (Genesove and Mayer, 1997). In fact, unless the supply elasticity of houses is very low (or zero), we expect the price effect due to a change in the demand for housing to be accompanied by a change in the number of transactions.As discussed in Section 3.3.1, we do not use a regression discontinuity approach to address the question of the change in the quantity of transactions. Fig. 3 confirms that this would produce no significant result. This figure shows the number of transactions relative to the threshold in each year. The figure is centered on zero, that is, the transactions at exactly 125% of the CLL. The figure shows no discontinuity in the number of transactions above and below the threshold.We consider the difference in the share of transactions in our sample that falls above and below the threshold in the year that the limit is in effect and in the subsequent year in a differences-in-differences setup. This test is equivalent to a T-test for the mean of the variable “Above the threshold”, which compares the average of this variable in the year that the limit is in effect (the “pre” year) and in the subsequent year. If the change in the CLL affects the quantity of transactions, we should see an increase in the share of observations above the threshold when the limit is raised, as credit becomes cheaper for those transactions.Download: Download high-res image (217KB)Download: Download full-size imageFig. 3. article.5. Ease of access to conforming loans an: Table 5 presents the results for our baseline specification of the effect on house prices. This table presents Fama–MacBeth coefficients from year-by-year regressions, as previously described in Section 3.3.1. The coefficient of interest in Panel A of Table 5 is that on the interaction variable, and it shows that houses above the threshold of the CLL divided by 0.8 transacted at a value per square foot that was lower by about $1.17 in the year that the CLL was in effect.The other coefficients on the regressions for value per square foot are consistent with what we know about house prices over this period. Houses above the threshold of 125% of the CLL (i.e., the more expensive houses in the regression sample) are associated with a higher average value per square foot. In unreported analyses, we find that more expensive houses are generally associated with a higher value per square foot (i.e., price rises quicker than house size in the whole distribution of transactions). This is also the case within the regression sample. Also, the “Year CLL” dummy variable is associated with a strong negative effect, reflecting the strong increase in house valuations that we saw in this period in the United States. Given that the year in which the CLL is in effect is always the “pre” year in the regressions, we expect those transactions, on average, to be associated with a lower value per square foot.In Table 5, Panel A, columns 2 and 3, we use the residuals from the regressions we described in Section 3.2 as the dependent variable to account for differences in quality between houses. The results are qualitatively similar to the ones in column 1. In column 2, we are using the residuals of a regression of the log of the house price on a set of characteristics, and we find a point estimate of −0.002, which translates into the residual being lower by $742 for houses above the threshold of 125% of the CLL when the CLL binds, considering an average transaction value of $371,000. This suggests that transactions that cannot be financed at 80% with conforming loans are made at lower prices even after we control for a rich set of house characteristics.Similarly, in column 3 of Panel A, Table 5, we confirm that even when we use the value per square foot as the dependent variable but control for house quality, the interaction term is significant and economically large even though the point estimate of $0.67 for houses above the threshold is slightly lower than the results in column 1, where we do not adjust for house quality. The difference between the point estimate of $1.17 and $0.67 in this specification indicates that houses above the limit are of somewhat worse quality than those below the limit in the year that the limit is in effect.Panel C of Table 5 shows that the results for all three valuation measures are qualitatively and quantitatively very similar when we run the same outcome variables in the pooled regression setup discussed in Section 3.3.1.Fig. 4 shows that the value per square foot levels off at the threshold of 125% of the CLL for the lowest income quartile of ZIP codes. This is consistent with transactions becoming particularly challenging for this subset of borrowers once prices are such that homes are harder to finance using a conforming loan.In Table 1 of the Online Appendix we show that our estimates are not driven by borrowers with very unusual LTV levels, namely those with LTV below 50 percent and above 80 percent. Borrowers with those choices of LTV are likely to either have access to abundant equity to put up when buying a home or to be very constrained and need a very high LTV. By constraining our sample to include only borrowers who choose a first lien LTV between 50 and 80 percent we capture the transactions that should be most affected by the conforming loan limit. The results are quantitatively similar to those we obtain for the whole sample, which reinforces our interpretation that the main results are caused by the CLL and not some other spurious factor.Download: Download high-res image (339KB)Download: Download full-size imageFig. 4. Treatment assignmentIn our main specification, we base the treatment assignment on actual transaction prices. This procedure could create the concern that treatment assignment could be correlated with the outcomes of interest (Kung, 2014). To address this, we construct predicted prices using hedonic regressions of house value on a number of house characteristics and use the estimated coefficients to predict house values at the time of transaction.To estimate the coefficients associated with each characteristic for each year, we use transactions within the last 12 months (rolling window for each transaction in the sample). Specifically, we estimate the following regressions by MSA and by year: (5)HouseValuei=θ0+ΘXi+monthi+ZIPi+ɛi.The set of controls Xi is a similar set of variables as the one used to estimate the residual in the hedonic regressions section, and all regressions include month and ZIP code fixed effects. We use the estimated coefficients from each regression and the house characteristics to estimate the predicted value of each house in the sample. Assignment of transactions into treatment and control groups is done using this predicted value rather than the actual transaction prices.In Panel B of Table 5, we run our main empirical specification using this alternative assignment. The results show that the main findings are economically very similar and of similar statistical significance. This indicates that the results are robust to the concern of selection into treatment assignment that may arise when using transaction values. We describe the sample used in the predicted transaction values specification in the Online Appendix.5.2. Falsification testsWe want to rule out that our results are driven by differences in secular trends between houses on either side of the threshold of 125% of the CLL. Specifically, if more expensive houses have, on average, lower house price growth from one year to the next relative to less expensive houses, we might obtain the results reported in Table 5, but we might also obtain similar results for samples with transactions above and below other arbitrary thresholds.To distinguish between the effect of true conforming loan limits and different trends along the distribution of houses, we run the same regressions described in Section 3.3.1 for placebo loan limits. We do this by shifting the true conforming loan limit in $10,000 steps from the true value each year. We start at CLL-$100,000 and move 20 steps until we reach CLL+$100,000. For each of these 21 tests, we first define the “shift” relative to the true conforming loan limits, and then we change the limits for all years by that amount. For example, when we are changing all the limits by −$20,000, this means that the “placebo” limit for 1999 is $220,000 instead of the true $240,000, the “placebo” limit for 2000 is $232,700 instead of $252,700, and so on. We then run the same year-by-year regressions and produce Fama–MacBeth coefficients for each of the 20 alternative “placebo” values for the CLL. Table 6 shows the results produced by this exercise.The table shows that the coefficients of interest we obtain for all three dependent variables (values per square foot, residuals from the transaction amounts, and residuals of values per square foot) are among the lowest of all those obtained with the 20 “placebo” trials (the ranking is given in the last two rows of the table). The coefficient for the value per square foot measure is the lowest of the 21 trials if we use the whole sample or if we limit our attention to a sample of transactions that all have an LTV between 0.5 and 0.8.9 The coefficients for the true conforming loan limits are the third and second lowest when we use the whole sample and the two residual measures from the hedonic regressions as the left-hand-side variables in the regressions.If we limit our attention to placebo limits that are below the true limits, the true conforming loan limits produce the lowest coefficients out of those trials in all, except one measure, for which it is the second lowest (transaction value residual). We consider these to be true “placebos”, because all the transactions used for those regressions are, by construction, below the “eligibility” criteria of 125% of the true conforming loan limit, both in the year that the limit is in effect and in the subsequent year. As such, credit availability for these transactions does not change from one year to the next.When we compute the standard deviation of those coefficients, we find that the coefficients using value per square foot as the dependent variable are statistically significantly different from the average of the other coefficients at a 5% level in both the whole sample and in the restricted sample with the LTV between 0.5 and 0.8. The fourth row of Table 6 records the t-statistics for these tests. The fact that the results are directionally the same when using all three left-hand-side variables, and that there is no “placebo” limit that consistently produces results that are as strong as the ones from the true limit, further confirms that our coefficients are not obtained by pure chance.We also show that our results are not driven by a “focal point effect”, whereby more bidders are attracted to bidding on houses just around the CLL. Fig. 5 illustrates bunching in the number of unobserved bidders for homes around the CLL. We measure this as the share of borrowers who apply for loans but ultimately either withdraw or do not use the loans they are approved for. If the CLL simply served as a focal point for home sales, we should expect more bidders for homes that are eligible for conforming loans. Instead, we find that our measure of the share of failed bids is lower, not higher, for borrowers who borrow up to the CLL. As observed in Fig. 3, the quantity of transactions reveals no significant jumps, and our proxy of failed offers for homes suggests that the effect we find on prices is more consistent with a cost of credit interpretation.Table 5. The last two rows show the ranking of the coefficient when we use the true CLL, first for all placebo limits and then when we only consider the placebo tests below the true CLL.5.3. Economic magnitude of the effectSection 2 discusses the user cost model and highlights the substantial disagreement surrounding the magnitude of house price elasticity to interest rates, as changes to the way a standard user cost model is specified can produce vastly different estimates. As we also discuss both in the introduction and then in more detail in Section 3.3, harder access to conforming loans above the threshold of 125% of the CLL translates into a higher weighted average cost of capital for buyers of those homes relative to homes below the threshold. Fig. 6 illustrates the evolution of the weighted average spread of the cost of an 80% LTV loan over the conforming loan rate for buyers with and without access to jumbo loans.To understand the magnitude of the effect of easier access to conforming loans on house prices, we use our estimate of a change in house prices around the CLL that ranges from 30.4 to 53.2 basis points (bps). We obtain a low of 30.4 bps when we use the residuals from the hedonic regressions with value per square foot as the dependent variable. The point estimate in the regressions is $0.67 in Table 5, and we scale this by the average value per square foot in the sample ($220/sq.ft., from the summary statistics table, Panel B, column 4) to obtain a 30.4 bp change. The high end of the estimate (53.2 bps) comes from the specification that uses the raw value per square foot as the dependent variable (point estimate of $1.17 from column (1) in Table 5, scaled by the same $220 per square foot from the summary statistics).As depicted in Fig. 6, the change in the cost of capital of an 80% loan for home buyers around the threshold of 1.25⋅CLL varies significantly depending on financing options and how far the price is from this threshold. If we focus on a borrower that moves from the left of the graph, where a home can be bought with an 80% conforming loan, to the point where the cost of capital increases by the jumbo-conforming spread (for borrowers with access to jumbo loans), we can compute the semi-elasticity of house prices to interest rates (the percentage change in prices divided by the change in interest rates). Our empirical strategy provides an unbiased local estimate of the semi-elasticity numerator, and we reference prior literature for the denominator estimate.An extensive literature provides estimates of the jumbo-conforming spread (see McKenzie, 2002, Ambrose et al., 2004, Sherlund, 2008, Kaufman, 2014, DeFusco and Paciorek, 2017). The most common estimates across all papers range from a low of 10 bps to a high of 24 bps. Kaufman (2014) obtains an estimate of 10 bps by using a regression discontinuity approach on the access to conforming loans around the threshold of the CLL divided by 0.8 in appraisal values. This estimate is particularly relevant for our purposes given that it explores the part of the distribution of homes that we also consider and thus represents the interest rate value closest to our estimates. For comparison, in our sample of transactions, borrowers who choose to obtain a conforming loan of less than 80% LTV above the threshold put up, on average, an additional $5000. If we assume that the spread of cost of the additional down payment over the conforming loan rate is 5 percentage points, this is equivalent to a spread of about 6–8 bps in the weighted average cost of financing for these borrowers relative to those who buy a house below the threshold. Assuming a spread of 5 percentage points over the conforming mortgage rate is reasonable given many borrowers resort to a jumbo loan even very close to the threshold of 125% of the CLL (as illustrated in Figure 2 of the Online Appendix). This suggests that the additional equity’s cost is substantial for some borrowers.If we divide the point estimates of house price changes due to the easier availability of conforming loans by the range in the jumbo-conforming spread, we obtain elasticities of house prices to interest rates that vary between 1.3 and 5.3. These estimates are necessarily very local in nature, that is, they use a narrow slice of the transactions in the data and do not take into account general equilibrium effects. They also focus on the change in the cost of funding for one particular type of borrower moving from a conforming loan to a jumbo loan. Still, they provide one of the first unbiased empirical estimates of this semi-elasticity in the literature, and the results are at the lower end of what has been previously proposed (see, e.g., Glaeser et al., 2013).
article.6. Conclusion: In this paper, we develop a difference-in-differences estimator that uses localized shocks to credit conditions induced by the yearly changes in the conforming loan limit (CLL). Our micro-level approach allows us to estimate the impact of access to conforming loans on house prices while controlling for changes in housing markets or economic conditions at a granular level. We find that a home that is easier to finance with a conforming loan due to an increase in the CLL has, on average, a $1.17 higher value per square foot compared to a closely comparable house that is just above the threshold for a conforming loan (with an 80% loan-to-value). The estimated magnitude of this difference is economically important — the average value per square foot of houses that transact around the CLL is $220, which means that an increase of $1.17 constitutes a 0.53% increase in prices.These results highlight that the CLL constitutes an important consideration for many households when deciding how much mortgage debt to take on. Most borrowers choose an LTV of 80% when they buy a home, but many deviate in order to stay below the conforming loan limit when the home crosses the threshold of 125% of the CLL. These borrowers are either unable to obtain a jumbo loan or find it cheaper to combine a larger down payment with a conforming loan at an LTV below 80%. As a result, these borrowers end up holding a larger fraction of equity in their house. This tendency of households to “stretch” around the CLL also highlights why one cannot use a simple RDD approach, and it underscores the need for the estimator we develop in this paper.Finally, our results suggest that the interest rate subsidy granted by the GSEs and, ultimately, the taxpayer not only benefits home buyers but partially accrues to homeowners (sellers) in the form of higher house prices. However, our estimated elasticity of mortgage rates to prices around the threshold we focus on is at the lower range of what has been previously proposed using time-series variation or estimates from major shocks to the housing market. This lower estimate might, in part, result from our approach’s ability to more effectively control for other factors that impact housing markets and are often correlated with interest rate changes.
